questions.stem: What is the approximate molar mass of NaCl? (Use H=1, C=12, N=14, O=16, Na=23, Cl=35.5, S=32, K=39, Ca=40, Mg=24, Fe=56, Ag=108)
questions.explanation: Summing the atomic masses in NaCl gives a molar mass of about 58.5 g/mol.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How do you find the molar mass of a compound?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Add the atomic masses of all atoms in the formula.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Multiply the atomic masses of all atoms in the formula.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use only the largest atomic mass in the formula.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Average the atomic masses of the atoms in the formula.

hints.rationale: Molar mass is the sum of the atomic masses of every atom in the formula unit.

hints.prompt: Using Na = 23 and Cl = 35.5, which setup gives the molar mass of NaCl?
hints.rationale: NaCl has one Na and one Cl, so add 23 + 35.5.

hints.prompt: One choice, 117 g/mol, is double the mass of one NaCl formula unit (as if it were counted twice). Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: 117 g/mol counts the formula unit twice; NaCl has just one Na and one Cl.

questions.stem: What is the molarity of a solution with 1 mol of solute in 1 L of solution?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which relationship gives the molarity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: molarity = moles of solute x liters of solution

choices.label: B
choices.text: molarity = moles of solute / liters of solution

choices.label: C
choices.text: molarity = liters of solution / moles of solute

choices.label: D
choices.text: molarity = moles of solute - liters of solution

hints.rationale: Molarity M = moles / liters.

hints.prompt: With 1 mol of solute in 1 L, which setup gives the molarity?
hints.rationale: Divide moles by liters: 1 / 1.

hints.prompt: One choice, 0.5 M, would require the 1 mol to be dissolved in 2 L instead of the given 1 L. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: 0.5 M corresponds to 1 mol in 2 L; the problem gives 1 L.

questions.stem: A reaction proceeds in two steps with ΔH1 = -400 kJ and ΔH2 = 250 kJ. If the overall reaction is the sum of these steps, what is the overall ΔH?
questions.explanation: By Hess's law, ΔH_overall = ΔH1 + ΔH2 = -400 + 250 = -150 kJ.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: By Hess's law, the overall enthalpy change of a multi-step reaction equals

choices.label: A
choices.text: the product of the step enthalpy changes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the difference divided by the number of steps.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the sum of the enthalpy changes of the steps.

choices.label: D
choices.text: only the largest step enthalpy change.

hints.rationale: Hess's law: the overall enthalpy is the sum of the step enthalpies.

hints.prompt: With ΔH1 = -400 kJ and ΔH2 = 250 kJ, which setup gives the overall ΔH?
hints.rationale: Add the two step enthalpies keeping their signs: -400 + 250.

hints.prompt: One choice, -650 kJ, comes from treating the +250 kJ step as -250 kJ (a sign error). Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: -650 kJ results from flipping the sign of the +250 kJ step.

questions.stem: An ideal fluid flows at 3 m/s through a pipe of cross-sectional area 4 m^2. What is the volume flow rate?
questions.explanation: Flow rate Q = A v = (4)(3) = 12 m^3/s.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which relationship gives the volume flow rate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: volume flow rate = cross-sectional area / flow speed

choices.label: B
choices.text: volume flow rate = flow speed / cross-sectional area

choices.label: C
choices.text: volume flow rate = cross-sectional area + flow speed

choices.label: D
choices.text: volume flow rate = cross-sectional area x flow speed

hints.rationale: For steady flow, Q = A x v.

hints.prompt: The pipe has area 4 m^2 and speed 3 m/s. Which setup gives the flow rate?
hints.rationale: Multiply area by speed; do not divide.

hints.prompt: One choice, 1.33 m^3/s, comes from dividing area by speed (4 / 3) rather than multiplying. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: 1.33 m^3/s reverses the relation (area / speed) and is not the flow rate.

questions.stem: What is the ground-state electron configuration of a neutral helium (Z=2) atom?
questions.explanation: Filling orbitals by the Aufbau principle gives helium: 1s2.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which principle tells you the order in which electrons fill orbitals in a ground-state atom?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The Aufbau principle: fill the lowest-energy orbitals first.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hund's rule of maximum multiplicity, used by itself.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The idea that an atom can hold only two electrons total.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The octet rule for main-group bonding.

hints.rationale: Ground-state configurations fill orbitals from lowest energy upward (Aufbau).

hints.prompt: Neutral helium has Z = 2, so how many electrons must the configuration account for?
hints.rationale: A neutral atom has electrons equal to its atomic number: 2 for helium.

hints.prompt: One choice, 1s2 2s2, accounts for 4 electrons - too many for helium. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: 1s2 2s2 describes 4 electrons; helium has only 2.

questions.stem: A net force of 20 N acts on a 3 kg object. What is its acceleration?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which relationship gives the acceleration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: acceleration = the net force x the mass

choices.label: B
choices.text: acceleration = the net force / the mass

choices.label: C
choices.text: acceleration = the mass / the net force

choices.label: D
choices.text: acceleration = the net force - the mass

hints.rationale: Newton's second law: a = F / m.

hints.prompt: A 20 N force acts on a 3 kg object. Which setup gives the acceleration?
hints.rationale: Divide force by mass: 20 / 3.

hints.prompt: One choice, 60 m/s^2, comes from multiplying force by mass (20 x 3) instead of dividing. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: Acceleration is F / m; multiplying gives 60.

questions.stem: How much energy is stored in a 1 μF capacitor charged to 5 V?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which expression gives the energy stored in a charged capacitor?
hints.rationale: Capacitor energy is U = (1/2) C V^2.

hints.prompt: With C = 1 μF and V = 5 V, which setup gives the stored energy?
hints.rationale: Substitute into (1/2) C V^2: (1/2)(1)(5)^2.

hints.prompt: One choice, 25 μJ, comes from forgetting the factor of 1/2 (using C V^2). Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: 25 μJ omits the 1/2 in (1/2) C V^2.

questions.stem: After 2 half-lives, what fraction of a radioactive sample remains?
questions.explanation: Each half-life halves the amount: fraction = (1/2)^2 = 0.25.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: After n half-lives, the fraction of a sample remaining is
hints.rationale: Each half-life multiplies the amount by 1/2, so the fraction is (1/2)^n.

hints.prompt: After 2 half-lives, which setup gives the fraction remaining?
hints.rationale: Two half-lives give (1/2)^2.

hints.prompt: One choice, 0.5, is the fraction remaining after only ONE half-life, not two. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: 0.5 corresponds to a single half-life; two half-lives leave less.

questions.stem: Metallic bonding is characterized by

choices.label: A
choices.text: positive ions in a sea of delocalized electrons.

choices.label: B
choices.text: electron pairs localized between two atoms.

choices.label: C
choices.text: full transfer of electrons to form a salt.

questions.explanation: In metals, valence electrons are delocalized over a lattice of cations, explaining conductivity.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Conductivity in metals requires charge carriers that can move freely. Which particles serve this role in a metal?
hints.rationale: Valence electrons are the mobile charge carriers in metals.

hints.prompt: In an ionic solid electrons are transferred, and in a covalent bond they are shared in pairs. Metallic bonding differs because its valence electrons are

choices.label: A
choices.text: free to move throughout the whole lattice

hints.rationale: Metallic valence electrons roam the lattice rather than staying between two atoms.

hints.prompt: One option, 'electron pairs localized between two atoms,' actually describes ordinary covalent bonding, not metallic bonding. Which choice is that trap?

choices.label: A
choices.text: positive ions in a sea of delocalized electrons.

choices.label: B
choices.text: electron pairs localized between two atoms.

choices.label: C
choices.text: full transfer of electrons to form a salt.

hints.rationale: Localized shared pairs define covalent bonds - a classic distractor here.

questions.stem: How many moles are in 117 g of NaCl? (molar mass 58.5 g/mol)
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which relationship gives the number of moles?

choices.label: A
choices.text: number of moles = the mass / the molar mass

choices.label: B
choices.text: number of moles = the mass x the molar mass

choices.label: C
choices.text: number of moles = the molar mass / the mass

choices.label: D
choices.text: number of moles = the mass - the molar mass

hints.rationale: moles = mass / molar mass.

hints.prompt: For 117 g of NaCl (molar mass 58.5 g/mol), which setup gives the moles?
hints.rationale: Divide mass by molar mass: 117 / 58.5.

hints.prompt: One choice, 0.5 mol, comes from dividing molar mass by mass (58.5 / 117) - the ratio flipped. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: 0.5 mol inverts the ratio; moles = mass / molar mass, not the reverse.

questions.stem: If 0.02 L of 2 M solution is diluted to 1 L, what is the new concentration?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which relationship governs dilution (adding solvent while moles stay constant)?
hints.rationale: Moles are conserved on dilution: M1 V1 = M2 V2.

hints.prompt: With M1 = 2 M, V1 = 0.02 L, and V2 = 1 L, which setup gives the new concentration M2?
One choice, 100 M, comes from swapping the volumes (using V1 in the denominator). Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: 100 M results from (2)(1)/0.02, i.e., the volumes swapped.

questions.stem: For a reaction, ΔH = -100 kJ and ΔS = -0.1 kJ/K. What is ΔG at 300 K?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which equation relates Gibbs free energy to enthalpy and entropy?
hints.rationale: The Gibbs relation is ΔG = ΔH - T ΔS.

hints.prompt: With ΔH = -100 kJ, T = 300 K, and ΔS = -0.1 kJ/K, which setup gives ΔG?
hints.rationale: Substitute into ΔH - TΔS, keeping ΔS negative.

hints.prompt: One choice, -130 kJ, comes from ignoring that ΔS is negative (subtracting 30 instead of adding it). Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: -130 kJ treats -TΔS as -30 rather than +30.

questions.stem: The second law of thermodynamics states that the total entropy of an isolated system
questions.explanation: Spontaneous processes increase the total entropy of an isolated system.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: The second law of thermodynamics concerns which quantity of an isolated system?
hints.rationale: The second law is a statement about entropy.

hints.prompt: For a spontaneous process in an isolated system, the total entropy tends to

choices.label: A
choices.text: change in one consistent direction over time, never spontaneously reversing

choices.label: D
choices.text: oscillate up and down in equal amounts

hints.rationale: Spontaneous processes drive entropy in a single direction, not back and forth.

hints.prompt: One option claims the total entropy 'is always zero,' confusing a fixed value with how entropy changes. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: Entropy is generally nonzero; 'always zero' is a distractor.

questions.stem: Which atom has the larger atomic radius, Li or K?

choices.label: C
choices.text: atomic radius shows no periodic trend

questions.explanation: K has the larger atomic radius: same group; radius increases down a group, so K (lower) is larger.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How does atomic radius change as you move DOWN a group in the periodic table?
hints.rationale: Atomic radius increases down a group as electron shells are added.

hints.prompt: Li and K are in the same group, with K positioned BELOW Li. By the group trend, K therefore has

choices.label: B
choices.text: more occupied electron shells than Li

choices.label: C
choices.text: exactly the same size as Li

hints.rationale: K is lower in the group, so it has an additional occupied shell compared with Li.

hints.prompt: One option claims atomic radius 'shows no periodic trend,' which contradicts the periodic table. Which choice is that trap?

choices.label: C
choices.text: atomic radius shows no periodic trend

hints.rationale: Atomic radius follows clear periodic trends; 'no trend' is a distractor.

questions.stem: What is the weight of a 5 kg object near Earth's surface? (g = 9.8 m/s^2)
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which relationship gives the weight?
hints.rationale: Weight W = m x g.

hints.prompt: For a 5 kg object with g = 9.8 m/s^2, which setup gives the weight?
hints.rationale: Multiply mass by g: (5)(9.8).

hints.prompt: One choice, 5 N, just repeats the mass value and ignores gravity. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: 5 N forgets to multiply by g; weight = m x g, not m alone.

questions.stem: How much work is required to move a charge of 2 C through a potential difference of 6 V?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which relationship gives the work (energy) to move a charge?

choices.label: A
choices.text: work (energy) to move a charge = charge / potential difference

choices.label: B
choices.text: work (energy) to move a charge = potential difference / charge

choices.label: C
choices.text: work (energy) to move a charge = charge x potential difference

choices.label: D
choices.text: work (energy) to move a charge = charge + potential difference

hints.rationale: Work W = q x V.

hints.prompt: To move 2 C through a 6 V potential difference, which setup gives the work?
hints.rationale: Multiply charge by voltage: (2)(6).

hints.prompt: One choice, 0.333 J, comes from dividing charge by voltage (2 / 6) instead of multiplying. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: Work is q x V, not q / V.

questions.stem: A sample starts with 80 g of a radioisotope whose half-life is 2 days. How much remains after 2 days?
questions.explanation: Number of half-lives = 2/2 = 1; remaining = 80 x (1/2)^1 = 40 g.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: After exactly one half-life, the amount of a radioisotope remaining is

choices.label: A
choices.text: one quarter of the starting amount

choices.label: B
choices.text: the same as the starting amount

choices.label: D
choices.text: one half of the starting amount

hints.rationale: One half-life leaves 1/2 of the original sample.

hints.prompt: The sample starts at 80 g with a 2-day half-life. After 2 days, how many half-lives have elapsed?
hints.rationale: Elapsed time / half-life = 2 / 2 = 1 half-life.

hints.prompt: One choice, 20 g, is what would remain after TWO half-lives, not one. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: 20 g corresponds to two half-lives; only one has passed.

questions.stem: According to VSEPR theory, what is the molecular geometry of CH4?
questions.explanation: CH4 adopts a tetrahedral geometry based on its bonding and lone-pair electron domains.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: In VSEPR theory, a molecule's geometry is determined by

choices.label: A
choices.text: the molar mass of the molecule

choices.label: C
choices.text: the number of neutrons in the central atom

choices.label: D
choices.text: the number of bonding and lone-pair electron domains on the central atom

hints.rationale: VSEPR geometry is set by the count of electron domains around the central atom.

hints.prompt: In CH4, the central carbon forms 4 bonds and has 0 lone pairs. How many electron domains is that?
hints.rationale: Four bonds plus zero lone pairs equal 4 electron domains.

hints.prompt: One option, 'trigonal pyramidal,' is the shape for a central atom with 3 bonds and 1 lone pair (like NH3), not CH4. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: Trigonal pyramidal requires a lone pair, which carbon in CH4 does not have.

questions.stem: What is the mass of 3 mol of H2O? (molar mass 18 g/mol)
questions.explanation: mass = moles x molar mass = 3 x 18 = 54 g.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which relationship gives the mass?

choices.label: A
choices.text: mass = number of moles x molar mass

choices.label: B
choices.text: mass = number of moles / molar mass

choices.label: C
choices.text: mass = molar mass / number of moles

choices.label: D
choices.text: mass = number of moles + molar mass

hints.rationale: mass = moles x molar mass.

hints.prompt: For 3 mol of H2O (molar mass 18 g/mol), which setup gives the mass?
hints.rationale: Multiply moles by molar mass: (3)(18).

hints.prompt: One choice, 0.167 g, comes from dividing moles by molar mass (3 / 18) instead of multiplying. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: Mass = moles x molar mass, not moles / molar mass.

questions.stem: A solution has pH = 3. What is its hydrogen ion concentration [H+]?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How is hydrogen ion concentration obtained from pH?
hints.rationale: The definition inverts to [H+] = 10^(-pH).

hints.prompt: For pH = 3, which setup gives [H+]?
hints.rationale: Substitute pH into 10^(-pH): 10^(-3).

hints.prompt: One choice, 1 x 10^-11 M, is actually the hydroxide concentration [OH-] (from 14 - pH), not [H+]. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: 1 x 10^-11 M corresponds to [OH-], a classic mix-up.

questions.stem: What is the equilibrium constant expression K for the reaction 2 SO2 + O2 ⇌ 2 SO3?
questions.explanation: K places products over reactants, each concentration raised to its coefficient: K = [SO3]^2 / ([SO2]^2[O2]).
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: For a gas-phase equilibrium, the equilibrium constant expression K is written as

choices.label: A
choices.text: reactants over products, with no exponents

choices.label: C
choices.text: products over reactants, each concentration raised to its stoichiometric coefficient

choices.label: D
choices.text: the largest concentration divided by the smallest

hints.rationale: K = [products]^coeff / [reactants]^coeff.

hints.prompt: For 2 SO2 + O2 ⇌ 2 SO3, which species belongs in the NUMERATOR of K, and with what exponent?
hints.rationale: The product SO3 goes on top, with an exponent equal to its coefficient (2).

hints.prompt: One option writes reactants over products with no exponents, reversing the rule. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: K places products over reactants WITH coefficients as exponents.

questions.stem: A system absorbs 600 J of heat and does 200 J of work on its surroundings. What is the change in its internal energy?
questions.explanation: First law: ΔU = Q - W = 600 - 200 = 400 J.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: The first law of thermodynamics (with work done BY the system) is
hints.rationale: Internal energy change equals heat added minus work done by the system.

hints.prompt: The system absorbs Q = 600 J and does W = 200 J of work. Which setup gives ΔU?
One choice, 800 J, comes from ADDING the work instead of subtracting it. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: Work done BY the system is subtracted: Q - W, not Q + W.

questions.stem: Which atom has the higher first ionization energy, Li or Cs?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ionization energy shows no periodic trend

questions.explanation: Li has the higher first ionization energy: same group; ionization energy decreases down a group, so Li is higher.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How does first ionization energy change as you move DOWN a group?

choices.label: A
choices.text: it decreases (an electron is easier to remove)

hints.rationale: Ionization energy decreases down a group.

hints.prompt: Li and Cs are in the same group, with Cs far BELOW Li. By the group trend, the atom that holds its outer electron more tightly is

choices.label: A
choices.text: the lower one, because it is larger

choices.label: B
choices.text: the higher one, whose valence electron is closer to the nucleus

choices.label: D
choices.text: impossible to compare within a group

hints.rationale: Higher in the group means smaller and a tighter hold, so a larger ionization energy.

hints.prompt: One option claims ionization energy 'shows no periodic trend,' contradicting periodicity. Which choice is that trap?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ionization energy shows no periodic trend

hints.rationale: Ionization energy follows clear group and period trends.

questions.stem: What is the momentum of a 3 kg object moving at 20 m/s?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which relationship gives the momentum?
hints.rationale: Momentum p = m x v.

hints.prompt: For a 3 kg object moving at 20 m/s, which setup gives the momentum?
hints.rationale: Multiply mass by velocity: (3)(20).

hints.prompt: One choice, 23 kg·m/s, comes from ADDING mass and velocity (3 + 20) instead of multiplying. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: Momentum is m x v, not m + v.

questions.stem: What happens between two objects carrying opposite electric charges?

choices.label: B
choices.text: They always share charge equally at a distance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They exert no force on each other.

questions.explanation: Opposite charges attract; like charges repel.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: The electrostatic (Coulomb) force between two charges depends on

choices.label: C
choices.text: the signs and magnitudes of the charges and the distance between them

hints.rationale: Coulomb's law depends on charge signs, magnitudes, and separation.

hints.prompt: Two charges of the SAME sign (like charges) will
hints.rationale: Like charges repel; this sets up the contrast with opposite charges.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They always share charge equally at a distance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They exert no force on each other.

questions.stem: Sound waves in air are
questions.explanation: Sound is a longitudinal pressure wave: the medium compresses and rarefies along the travel direction.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Waves are classified by particle motion relative to travel. A LONGITUDINAL wave has particle motion that is

choices.label: A
choices.text: perpendicular to the direction of travel

choices.label: B
choices.text: in a circle around the source

choices.label: D
choices.text: parallel to the direction the wave travels

hints.rationale: In a longitudinal wave, particles oscillate along the travel direction.

hints.prompt: Sound moves through air by compressing and rarefying it. Which statement about sound is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the medium's particles oscillate back and forth along the travel direction

choices.label: B
choices.text: the medium's particles oscillate at right angles to travel

choices.label: C
choices.text: no medium is required for it to travel

choices.label: D
choices.text: it can only exist as a standing wave

hints.prompt: One option calls sound 'transverse,' but transverse means particle motion perpendicular to travel (like a wave on a string). Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: Sound's particles move along, not across, the travel direction, so transverse is wrong.

questions.stem: What is the approximate bond angle in CH4 (tetrahedral)?
questions.explanation: A tetrahedral molecule such as CH4 has bond angles of 109.5 degrees.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: A molecule's bond angle is set primarily by its
hints.rationale: Bond angles follow from the arrangement of electron domains (geometry).

hints.prompt: CH4 has a tetrahedral electron geometry (4 bonding domains, no lone pairs). Four domains spread out to

choices.label: B
choices.text: the maximum symmetric separation possible in three dimensions

hints.rationale: Four equivalent domains point to the corners of a tetrahedron, spreading as far apart as possible.

hints.prompt: One choice, 120 degrees, is the bond angle for a FLAT trigonal planar molecule (3 domains), not tetrahedral CH4. Which choice is that trap?
hints.rationale: 120 degrees belongs to trigonal planar geometry; CH4 is tetrahedral.

questions.stem: How many molecules are in 0.5 mol of a substance? (Avogadro's number = 6.022 x 10^23 /mol)
questions.explanation: molecules = moles x N_A = 0.5 x 6.022 x 10^23 = 3.01 x 10^23.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which relationship gives the number of molecules?

choices.label: A
choices.text: number of molecules = number of moles x Avogadro's number (6.022 x 10^23 /mol)

choices.label: B
choices.text: number of molecules = number of moles / Avogadro's number (6.022 x 10^23 /mol)

choices.label: C
